title: P&O Cruises Antarctica Cruises 2026
intro: P&O Cruises, Britain's most iconic cruise line with over 180 years of maritime heritage, offers an extraordinary gateway to the White Continent through its partnership with expedition specialists. Unlike typical P&O sailings, Antarctica voyages aboard the purpose-built expedition vessel MS Borealis combine the line's signature British hospitality with genuine polar exploration. Guests experience Zodiac landings among penguin colonies, expert-led lectures from naturalists and glaciologists, and the rare privilege of crossing the Antarctic Circle. These 14-21 night expeditions depart from Buenos Aires or Ushuaia, featuring extended sea days across the Drake Passage rewarded with pristine icescapes, whale encounters, and shore landings at research stations. P&O's Antarctica program emphasizes environmental stewardship while delivering comfortable accommodation, fine dining, and enrichment programs that transform passengers into informed polar ambassadors.

faqs.question: How much does a P&O Cruises Antarctica cruise cost?
faqs.answer: P&O Cruises Antarctica expeditions range from EUR 4,899 to EUR 12,000+ per person depending on season, duration, and cabin category. Inside cabins on 14-night voyages start around EUR 4,899 in November, rising to EUR 5,699 for December peak dates. Balcony cabins essential for polar scenery viewing cost EUR 6,299-EUR 7,499, while suites with butler service range EUR 9,499-EUR 11,299. The 21-night Falklands-South Georgia-Antarctica itinerary commands premiums of EUR 8,299-EUR 14,000. Prices include flights from London, pre-cruise hotel, all meals, drinks with dinner, excursions, and polar parka. Kayaking adds EUR 299-499, camping EUR 399, and premium drinks packages EUR 49-69 daily. Solo travellers face 75-100% single supplements though occasional no-supplement sales appear.

faqs.question: Which P&O Cruises ships sail to Antarctica?
faqs.answer: P&O Cruises deploys its expedition-capable sister ships MS Borealis and MS Bolette for Antarctica voyages. These 1,360 and 1,338-guest vessels entered P&O service in 2021 after extensive polar upgrades including strengthened ice-class hulls rated 1C, advanced stabilisation for Drake Passage crossings, and expanded mudrooms for Zodiac operations. MS Borealis carries 12 Zodiacs with capacity for all guests to land twice daily when conditions permit. Both ships feature dedicated expedition lounges, scientific equipment including hydrophones for whale listening, and enrichment spaces for polar briefings. Unlike P&O's larger Britannia or Iona, these mid-size ships can navigate Antarctic fjords and operate flexible itineraries responding to ice conditions. The line rotates vessels seasonally with one ship typically dedicated to Antarctica November-March while the other operates Caribbean or world cruise segments.

faqs.question: When is best time to cruise Antarctica?
faqs.answer: The Antarctic cruise season runs November through March with distinct advantages each month. November offers pristine snow conditions, dramatic ice formations, and courting penguin behaviour with prices 10-15% below peak; however temperatures remain coldest and some landing sites may be ice-locked. December-January represents peak season with 20+ hours daylight, hatching penguin chicks, active seal colonies, and most reliable weather for Drake Passage crossings—expect highest prices and demand. February brings whale migration peaks with humpback and orca concentrations, moulting penguin chicks creating chaotic colonies, and slightly warmer conditions ideal for polar plunges; ice recedes permitting southernmost navigation. March offers dramatic sunsets, aurora possibilities, and significant savings though daylight diminishes and wildlife begins dispersing. P&O schedules primarily December-February departures optimising wildlife and conditions for British passengers seeking midwinter escapes.

faqs.question: What is included?
faqs.answer: P&O Antarctica cruises include substantial value compared to luxury expedition competitors. All meals span main dining room, buffet, and specialty restaurants including The Beach House and Sindhu; house wine, beer, and soft drinks accompany lunch and dinner complimentary. All Zodiac excursions and shore landings are included with no daily limit—guests typically land twice daily when conditions permit. The expedition team of naturalists, historians, and photographers delivers comprehensive enrichment through lectures, deck commentary, and guided walks. Each guest receives a quality polar parka to retain. Port taxes, fees, and basic WiFi are covered. Select sailings include return flights from London Gatwick to Buenos Aires or Ushuaia with overnight hotel accommodation. Gratuities, premium drinks, spa services, specialty activities like kayaking and camping, satellite internet packages, and travel insurance remain additional. P&O's British-inclusive model eliminates nickel-and-diming common on American competitors.

faqs.question: Which ports?
faqs.answer: P&O Antarctica itineraries emphasise expedition landings over traditional port calls. Voyages typically embark Buenos Aires for overnight hotel stays exploring Argentina's capital, or Ushuaia—the world's southernmost city—for immediate Antarctic access. The Antarctic Peninsula region features 15-20 potential landing sites including Neko Harbour for gentoo colonies, Paradise Bay's glaciers, Port Lockroy's historic post office, and Lemaire Channel's dramatic narrows. Deception Island offers volcanic beaches and industrial archaeology. Extended 21-night itineraries incorporate the Falkland Islands for Magellanic penguins and British culture at Stanley, plus South Georgia—wildlife paradise hosting 250,000 king penguins, elephant seals, and Shackleton's grave at Grytviken. No two voyages follow identical routes; expedition leaders adjust daily based on ice conditions, wildlife activity, and weather to maximise experiences. Zodiac cruising substitutes when landing conditions prove unsafe, offering intimate ice and marine mammal encounters impossible from larger vessels.

faqs.question: How far ahead to book?
faqs.answer: Antarctica's limited season and P&O's single-ship deployment create exceptional demand requiring 12-18 month advance booking for optimal cabin selection and pricing. Early booking discounts of 10-15% typically apply to reservations made 12+ months before departure. Balcony cabins—essential for private polar viewing—sell first, particularly mid-ship locations with minimal motion. Solo travellers face particular urgency as single-occupancy cabins number under 20 per ship. Final payment falls 90 days before sailing with cancellation penalties escalating from deposit loss to 100% fare. Last-minute availability occasionally emerges 60-90 days out through cancellations, though restricted to inside cabins at full price. Travel agents specialising in expedition cruising access group allocations and waitlist priority. P&O's Cruise Personaliser opens 90 days pre-departure for shore excursion bookings, dining reservations, and special requests; early access proves valuable for limited-capacity kayaking and camping experiences.

faqs.question: Good for families?
faqs.answer: P&O Antarctica cruises present mixed family suitability depending on children's ages and expedition readiness. Minimum age is typically 8 years for Antarctic Treaty compliance and Zodiac safety, though P&O recommends 10+ for meaningful engagement. Unlike P&O's family-focused Caribbean sailings, Antarctica voyages lack dedicated kids' clubs, waterslides, or teen discos—instead offering junior expedition programs where children join naturalists for penguin counts and ice photography workshops. Sea days crossing the Drake Passage demand self-entertainment during potentially rough conditions. The educational dimension profoundly impacts receptive children, creating transformative wildlife encounters and citizenship certificates for Antarctic visits. Family cabins are limited; connecting rooms and triple-occupancy suites require immediate booking. Pricing presents significant barriers—children pay full fare with no discounts, and single-parent families face punitive supplements. Families prioritising togetherness, education, and shared adventure over traditional cruise entertainment find exceptional value; those seeking childcare and children's facilities should consider P&O's summer Mediterranean or Caribbean alternatives.

faqs.question: Cancellation policy?
faqs.answer: P&O Cruises Antarctica bookings carry stringent cancellation terms reflecting expedition operational costs and limited seasonality. Standard deposits range EUR 500-1,000 per person due at booking. Cancellation 181+ days before departure forfeits deposit only. Between 180-91 days, 30% of cruise fare is retained. From 90-61 days, 50% is lost. Within 60-31 days, 75% penalty applies. Final 30 days incurs 100% forfeiture with no refund—travel insurance with cancel-for-any-reason coverage is essential given Antarctica's unpredictability. P&O offers Future Cruise Credit options for cancellations 90+ days out, transferable to alternative sailings within 24 months minus EUR 100 administration fee. Medical documentation waives standard penalties for documented illness or bereavement. Name changes incur EUR 150 per person. P&O reserves right to cancel itineraries due to ice conditions or mechanical issues, offering full refunds or alternative voyages without compensation for consequential costs like flights or hotels—separate travel insurance covering these expenses is strongly advised given expedition cruising's inherent uncertainties.
